domain:
An mRNA vaccine teaches the immune system by introducing a small piece of genetic material called messenger RNA (mRNA) into the body. This mRNA contains the instructions for making a specific viral protein, which is a key component of the virus that causes the disease.

When the mRNA is injected into the body, it is taken up by cells called antigen-presenting cells (APCs), such as dendritic cells and macrophages. These cells read the mRNA instructions and produce the viral protein.

The APCs then display the viral protein on their surface, along with other molecules called co-stimulatory signals. This signals

neighbor:
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the genetic material that carries the instructions for the development, growth, and functioning of an organism. RNA (ribonucleic acid) is a molecule that plays a crucial role in protein synthesis, which is the process by which cells make proteins. The process by which DNA is transcribed into RNA is called transcription.

Here is a step-by-step description of how DNA is transcribed into RNA in a cell:
